narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is above the typical student's 3.21—and your impressive LSAT score of 43, well above the average of 36.70. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 77.4% chance that you would not pass, leading to the tough decision to reject your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school: your Year 3 decile of 2 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it stands notably below the average of 5.53 and signals challenges in maintaining high performance later in your studies. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also well below the typical 5.71, was the next most influential, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic standing. While your upper-middle family income bracket nudged the prediction slightly toward failure compared to many applicants, it played a smaller role. On a brighter note, your strong LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success, and your choice of full-time study aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%) who succeed this way. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by excelling in test scores and GPA but lagging in school deciles, which outweighed those positives in the model's eyes for bar exam success. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—many with similar backgrounds overcome these hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as those were the top hurdles here, and reapply once you've strengthened that foundation. You're capable of turning this around, and we're rooting for your next steps toward passing the bar and practicing law. Keep pushing—you've got this.
